Musical artist

**Joan Jett** (born **Joan Marie Larkin**; September 22, 1958[[1]](#cite_note-Rolling_Stone-1)) is an American singer, songwriter, guitarist, businesswoman, and actress. Often referred to as the "[Godmother of Punk](/wiki/Honorific_nicknames_in_popular_music#J "Honorific nicknames in popular music")", she is regarded as a [rock icon](/wiki/Pop_icon "Pop icon") and an influential figure in [popular rock music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music").

Jett co-founded and performed with [the Runaways](/wiki/The_Runaways "The Runaways") from 1975 to 1979, with whom she released [four albums](/wiki/The_Runaways#Discography "The Runaways"). After their dissolution, she recorded her debut album and brought it to several [record labels](/wiki/Record_label "Record label") with producer [Kenny Laguna](/wiki/Kenny_Laguna "Kenny Laguna"), all of whom rejected her. As a result, she formed her own [independent record label](/wiki/Independent_record_label "Independent record label") [Blackheart Records](/wiki/Blackheart_Records "Blackheart Records") with Laguna. Her 1980 [self-titled debut album](/wiki/Bad_Reputation_(Joan_Jett_album) "Bad Reputation (Joan Jett album)") (later [reissued](/wiki/Reissue "Reissue") as *Bad Reputation*) became her first entry onto the US [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200"), bolstered by the attention its [title track](/wiki/Bad_Reputation_(Joan_Jett_song) "Bad Reputation (Joan Jett song)") received.

Following the success of her debut record, Jett founded her backing band, [Joan Jett and the Blackhearts](/wiki/Joan_Jett_and_the_Blackhearts "Joan Jett and the Blackhearts"). Their 1981 album, [*I Love Rock 'n Roll*](/wiki/I_Love_Rock_%27n_Roll_(album) "I Love Rock 'n Roll (album)"), peaked at number two on the US *Billboard* 200. Its lead single, "[I Love Rock 'n Roll](/wiki/I_Love_Rock_%27n%27_Roll#Joan_Jett_version "I Love Rock 'n' Roll")", topped the US [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") and many international charts; it became Jett's [signature song](/wiki/List_of_signature_songs "List of signature songs") and was inducted into the [Grammy Hall of Fame](/wiki/List_of_Grammy_Hall_of_Fame_Award_recipients_(E%E2%80%93I) "List of Grammy Hall of Fame Award recipients (E–I)") in 2016. The album also produced her second top ten single "[Crimson and Clover](/wiki/Crimson_and_Clover#Joan_Jett_and_the_Blackhearts_cover_version "Crimson and Clover")".

Jett has released [eleven more albums](/wiki/Joan_Jett_discography#Albums "Joan Jett discography"), of which [*Album*](/wiki/Album_(Joan_Jett_album) "Album (Joan Jett album)") (1983) and [*Up Your Alley*](/wiki/Up_Your_Alley_(album) "Up Your Alley (album)") (1988) earned gold and platinum [certifications](/wiki/Music_recording_certification "Music recording certification"), respectively.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Her 1988 single "[I Hate Myself for Loving You](/wiki/I_Hate_Myself_for_Loving_You "I Hate Myself for Loving You")" netted Jett her third top ten single on the Hot 100 and her first [Grammy](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards") nomination for [Best Rock Performance](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Rock_Performance_by_a_Duo_or_Group_with_Vocal "Grammy Award for Best Rock Performance by a Duo or Group with Vocal"). Other Hot 100 singles include "Fake Friends", "Good Music", "[Light of Day](/wiki/Light_of_Day_(song) "Light of Day (song)")", "Little Liar", and the covers "[Do You Wanna Touch Me (Oh Yeah)](/wiki/Do_You_Wanna_Touch_Me#Joan_Jett_version "Do You Wanna Touch Me")", "[Dirty Deeds](/wiki/Dirty_Deeds_Done_Dirt_Cheap_(song)#Joan_Jett_cover "Dirty Deeds Done Dirt Cheap (song)")", and "[Everyday People](/wiki/Everyday_People#Notable_versions "Everyday People")".[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Jett was included on *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")*'s 2003 and 2023 lists of the greatest guitarists of all time. In 2015, she and the Blackhearts were inducted into the [Rock and Roll Hall of Fame](/wiki/Rock_and_Roll_Hall_of_Fame "Rock and Roll Hall of Fame"). Outside of music, she headlined the film *[Light of Day](/wiki/Light_of_Day "Light of Day")* in 1987 and has appeared in several television series. She is also recognized for her activism and persona.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joan_Jett&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Joan Marie Larkin was born on September 22, 1958,[[1]](#cite_note-Rolling_Stone-1) at [Lankenau Hospital](/wiki/Lankenau_Medical_Center "Lankenau Medical Center") in [Wynnewood, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Wynnewood,_Pennsylvania "Wynnewood, Pennsylvania"), a suburb of [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), to James and Dorothy Larkin.[[6]](#cite_note-6) She is the oldest of three children. Her father was an insurance salesman and her mother was a secretary.[[1]](#cite_note-Rolling_Stone-1) Her family was [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant"), attending church and Sunday school, but were not strict in their religious beliefs.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In 1967, Jett's family moved to [Rockville, Maryland](/wiki/Rockville,_Maryland "Rockville, Maryland"), where she attended Randolph Junior High School and [Wheaton High School](/wiki/Wheaton_High_School "Wheaton High School").[[8]](#cite_note-8) She got her first guitar at the age of 13.[[9]](#cite_note-9) She took some guitar lessons, but soon quit because the instructor kept trying to teach her [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") songs.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Her family then moved to [West Covina, California](/wiki/West_Covina,_California "West Covina, California"), in [Los Angeles County](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County,_California "Los Angeles County, California"), providing Jett with the opportunity to pursue her musical interests. Shortly after the move, her parents divorced and she changed her name to Joan Jett, because she thought it had more of a rock star sound than her birth name. She has admitted in recent years that "Jett" was not actually her mother's maiden name, even though that is what she used to tell people.[[11]](#cite_note-11) In Los Angeles, Jett's favorite night spot was [Rodney Bingenheimer's English Disco](/wiki/Rodney_Bingenheimer%27s_English_Disco "Rodney Bingenheimer's English Disco"),[[12]](#cite_note-12) a venue that provided the [glam rock](/wiki/Glam_rock "Glam rock") style she loved.[[13]](#cite_note-VH1-13)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joan_Jett&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### 1970s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joan_Jett&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1970s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/The_Runaways_at_Brumrock_%2776.JPG/250px-The_Runaways_at_Brumrock_%2776.JPG)](/wiki/File:The_Runaways_at_Brumrock_%2776.JPG)

Jett (left) and [Cherie Currie](/wiki/Cherie_Currie "Cherie Currie") of The Runaways performing at Brumrock '76, [Bingley Hall](/wiki/Bingley_Hall "Bingley Hall"), Birmingham, UK, September 25, 1976

At age 16, Jett became a founding member of [the Runaways](/wiki/The_Runaways "The Runaways") with drummer [Sandy West](/wiki/Sandy_West "Sandy West"). After the brief tenure of [Micki Steele](/wiki/Michael_Steele_(musician) "Michael Steele (musician)"), who sang and played bass, [Jackie Fox](/wiki/Jackie_Fox "Jackie Fox"), [Lita Ford](/wiki/Lita_Ford "Lita Ford"), and [Cherie Currie](/wiki/Cherie_Currie "Cherie Currie") soon joined to complete the band which created the classic lineup. While Currie initially fronted the band, Jett shared some lead vocals, played rhythm guitar, and wrote or co-wrote some of the band's material along with Ford, West, and Currie. This lineup recorded three albums.

The band toured around the world and became an opening act for [Cheap Trick](/wiki/Cheap_Trick "Cheap Trick"), [Ramones](/wiki/Ramones "Ramones"), [Van Halen](/wiki/Van_Halen "Van Halen"), and [Tom Petty and the Heartbreakers](/wiki/Tom_Petty_and_the_Heartbreakers "Tom Petty and the Heartbreakers"). They found success abroad, especially in Japan. While the Runaways were popular in Europe, Asia, Australia, Canada, and South America, they could not garner the same level of success in the United States.[[13]](#cite_note-VH1-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) After Currie left the band, the band released two more albums with Jett handling the lead vocals: *[Waitin' for the Night](/wiki/Waitin%27_for_the_Night "Waitin' for the Night")* and *[And Now... The Runaways](/wiki/And_Now..._The_Runaways "And Now... The Runaways")*. While touring England with the Runaways in 1976, Jett first heard the song "[I Love Rock 'n' Roll](/wiki/I_Love_Rock_%27n%27_Roll "I Love Rock 'n' Roll")" when she saw Arrows perform it on their weekly UK television series *[Arrows](/wiki/Arrows_(TV_series) "Arrows (TV series)")*.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Altogether, they produced five albums from 1975 until they disbanded in the spring of 1979.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

In 1979, she returned to Los Angeles, where she began fulfilling an obligation of the Runaways to complete a film that was loosely based on the band's career entitled *We're All Crazee Now!* Three actresses stood in for the departed band members, including [Rainbeaux Smith](/wiki/Rainbeaux_Smith "Rainbeaux Smith"), who was also a rock drummer.[[17]](#cite_note-MTV-17) The plug was pulled on the project halfway through shooting after Jett fell ill, but in 1984, after she became famous, producers looked for a way to use the footage from the incomplete film.[[17]](#cite_note-MTV-17) Parts of the original footage of Jett were eventually used in another project, an underground film called *Du-beat-eo*, which was produced by Alan Sacks, but not commercially released.[[17]](#cite_note-MTV-17)

In 1979, Jett was in England pursuing a solo career. She recorded three songs there with the [Sex Pistols](/wiki/Sex_Pistols "Sex Pistols")' [Paul Cook](/wiki/Paul_Cook "Paul Cook") and [Steve Jones](/wiki/Steve_Jones_(musician) "Steve Jones (musician)"), one of which was an early version of [Arrows](/wiki/Arrows_(British_band) "Arrows (British band)")' "[I Love Rock 'n' Roll](/wiki/I_Love_Rock_%27n%27_Roll "I Love Rock 'n' Roll")". This version appears on the 1993 compilation album *[Flashback](/wiki/Flashback_(Joan_Jett_album) "Flashback (Joan Jett album)")*.[[18]](#cite_note-18) While working on *We're All Crazee Now!*, Jett met songwriter and producer [Kenny Laguna](/wiki/Kenny_Laguna "Kenny Laguna"), who was hired by her manager Toby Mamis to help Jett with writing some [tracks for the film](/wiki/Soundtrack "Soundtrack").[[17]](#cite_note-MTV-17) They became friends and decided to work together and Jett relocated to [Long Beach, New York](/wiki/Long_Beach,_New_York "Long Beach, New York"), where Laguna was based.

Soon after, Jett produced the [Germs](/wiki/Germs_(band) "Germs (band)")' only album, *[(GI)](/wiki/(GI) "(GI)")*.[[13]](#cite_note-VH1-13) Jett produced several bands prior to releasing her debut, and her label Blackheart Records released recordings from varied artists such as [thrash metal](/wiki/Thrash_metal "Thrash metal") band [Metal Church](/wiki/Metal_Church "Metal Church") and [rapper](/wiki/Rapping "Rapping") [Big Daddy Kane](/wiki/Big_Daddy_Kane "Big Daddy Kane").

### 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joan_Jett&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1980s")]

Jett and Laguna entered [the Who](/wiki/The_Who "The Who")'s [Ramport Studios](/wiki/Ramport_Studios "Ramport Studios") with the latter at the helm, and Jett's self-titled solo debut was released by [Ariola Records](/wiki/Ariola_Records "Ariola Records") in Europe on May 17, 1980. In the US, after the album was rejected by 23 major labels,[[19]](#cite_note-19) Jett and Laguna released it independently on their new [Blackheart Records](/wiki/Blackheart_Records "Blackheart Records") label, which they started with Laguna's daughter's college savings. Laguna remembers, "We couldn't think of anything else to do but print up records ourselves."[[17]](#cite_note-MTV-17) With Laguna's assistance, Jett formed [the Blackhearts](/wiki/Joan_Jett_and_the_Blackhearts "Joan Jett and the Blackhearts"). It has undergone many lineup changes since its inception, with Jett and Laguna being its only consistent members.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Jett_Rocks.jpg/250px-Jett_Rocks.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jett_Rocks.jpg)

Jett performing live with the [Gibson Melody Maker](/wiki/Gibson_Melody_Maker "Gibson Melody Maker") in Norway, during the 1980s

Jett's first appearance on film is in the 1981 live concert film *[Urgh! A Music War](/wiki/Urgh!_A_Music_War "Urgh! A Music War")*, performing "Bad Reputation" with the Blackhearts at [the Ritz](/wiki/The_Ritz_(rock_club) "The Ritz (rock club)") in New York City. A spring 1981 concert at [the Palladium](/wiki/Palladium_(New_York_City) "Palladium (New York City)") in New York City proved to be a turning point. Described by music journalists as a career-defining performance by Jett, it helped solidify a strong New York City following for Joan Jett and the Blackhearts.[[20]](#cite_note-20) After a year of touring and recording, the Blackhearts recorded a new album entitled *[I Love Rock 'n Roll](/wiki/I_Love_Rock_%27n_Roll_(album) "I Love Rock 'n Roll (album)")* for the label. Ambel was replaced by local guitarist [Ricky Byrd](/wiki/Ricky_Byrd "Ricky Byrd") during the recording. Byrd recalled in an interview with Guitarhoo!, "One day I went to a studio to jam around a bit with Jett and everything clicked".[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) The first single from the album was the title track, "[I Love Rock 'n' Roll](/wiki/I_Love_Rock_%27n%27_Roll#Joan_Jett_version "I Love Rock 'n' Roll")", which in the first half of 1982 was number one on the [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") for seven weeks in a row.[[23]](#cite_note-23) It is *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")*'s No. 56 song of all time[[24]](#cite_note-24) and has also been inducted into the [Grammy Hall of Fame](/wiki/Grammy_Hall_of_Fame "Grammy Hall of Fame") in 2016.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Jett released *[Album](/wiki/Album_(Joan_Jett_album) "Album (Joan Jett album)")* (1983) and *[Glorious Results of a Misspent Youth](/wiki/Glorious_Results_of_a_Misspent_Youth "Glorious Results of a Misspent Youth")* (1984). A string of [Top 40](/wiki/Top_40 "Top 40") hits followed, as well as sellout tours with [the Police](/wiki/The_Police "The Police"), [Queen](/wiki/Queen_(band) "Queen (band)"), and [Aerosmith](/wiki/Aerosmith "Aerosmith"), among others. She was among the first English-speaking rock acts to appear in [Panama](/wiki/Panama "Panama") and the [Dominican Republic](/wiki/Dominican_Republic "Dominican Republic").[[26]](#cite_note-26) According to Jett and Laguna, a riot occurred during their visit to Panama and [Manuel Noriega](/wiki/Manuel_Noriega "Manuel Noriega") requested Jett spend the night with him at the Presidential Palace.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Jett next beat out a number of contenders to star in the movie *[Light of Day](/wiki/Light_of_Day "Light of Day")* (1987) alongside [Michael J. Fox](/wiki/Michael_J._Fox "Michael J. Fox") as siblings who perform in a rock band. It was originally written by director [Paul Schrader](/wiki/Paul_Schrader "Paul Schrader") with [Bruce Springsteen](/wiki/Bruce_Springsteen "Bruce Springsteen") in mind; although Springsteen declined the role, he wrote the song "[Light of Day](/wiki/Light_of_Day_(song) "Light of Day (song)")" for the film.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) While the film received mixed reviews, Jett's performance was critically acclaimed.[[31]](#cite_note-31) [Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") of the *[Chicago Sun-Times](/wiki/Chicago_Sun-Times "Chicago Sun-Times")* believed she matched her acting to co-star [Gena Rowlands](/wiki/Gena_Rowlands "Gena Rowlands"), calling it "the most surprisingly good performance."[[32]](#cite_note-32) [Janet Maslin](/wiki/Janet_Maslin "Janet Maslin") of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* wrote: "Miss Jett is good too, snapping her way angrily through confrontational scenes and musical ones alike, and taking a sentimental turn just when the story does."[[33]](#cite_note-33)

The next year, her next album, *[Up Your Alley](/wiki/Up_Your_Alley_(album) "Up Your Alley (album)")* (1988), went multi-platinum. This album contains the single "I Hate Myself for Loving You", which peaked at No. 8 on the *Billboard* Hot 100 chart,[[34]](#cite_note-34) and had been used as the theme song for Sunday Night Football NFL games in America (with altered lyrics, by two singers) during the 2006 and 2007 seasons. Jett co-wrote the song "[House of Fire](/wiki/House_of_Fire "House of Fire")", which appeared on [Alice Cooper](/wiki/Alice_Cooper "Alice Cooper")'s 1989 album *[Trash](/wiki/Trash_(Alice_Cooper_album) "Trash (Alice Cooper album)")*.

### 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joan_Jett&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1990s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Joan_Jett_-_1994_-_02.jpg/250px-Joan_Jett_-_1994_-_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joan_Jett_-_1994_-_02.jpg)

Jett performing live at the [Bumbershoot](/wiki/Bumbershoot "Bumbershoot") festival, in Seattle, Washington, 1994

Jett's next album was *[The Hit List](/wiki/The_Hit_List_(Joan_Jett_album) "The Hit List (Joan Jett album)")*, which was an album consisting of cover songs. In 1990, the Blackhearts had a song on [*Days of Thunder'*s soundtrack](/wiki/Days_of_Thunder_(soundtrack) "Days of Thunder (soundtrack)"), "Long Live the Night", written by Jett with Randy Cantor and [Michael Caruso](/wiki/Michael_Caruso_(musician) "Michael Caruso (musician)"). Her 1991 release, *[Notorious](/wiki/Notorious_(Joan_Jett_album) "Notorious (Joan Jett album)")*, which featured [the Replacements](/wiki/The_Replacements_(band) "The Replacements (band)")' [Paul Westerberg](/wiki/Paul_Westerberg "Paul Westerberg") and former [Billy Idol](/wiki/Billy_Idol "Billy Idol") bass player Phil Feit, was the last with [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony")/[CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS"), as Jett switched to [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Records "Warner Bros. Records") In 1993, Jett and Laguna released *[Flashback](/wiki/Flashback_(Joan_Jett_album) "Flashback (Joan Jett album)")*, a compilation of various songs on their own [Blackheart Records](/wiki/Blackheart_Records "Blackheart Records"). In 1994, the Blackhearts released the well-received *[Pure and Simple](/wiki/Pure_and_Simple_(Joan_Jett_album) "Pure and Simple (Joan Jett album)")*, which featured tracks written with [Babes in Toyland's](/wiki/Babes_in_Toyland_(band) "Babes in Toyland (band)") [Kat Bjelland](/wiki/Kat_Bjelland "Kat Bjelland"), [L7](/wiki/L7_(band) "L7 (band)")'s [Donita Sparks](/wiki/Donita_Sparks "Donita Sparks") and [Bikini Kill](/wiki/Bikini_Kill "Bikini Kill")'s [Kathleen Hanna](/wiki/Kathleen_Hanna "Kathleen Hanna").

Jett returned to producing for the band [Circus Lupus](/wiki/Circus_Lupus "Circus Lupus") in 1992 and again, in 1994, for [Bikini Kill](/wiki/Bikini_Kill "Bikini Kill"). This recording was the *[New Radio +2](/wiki/Rebel_Girl_(Bikini_Kill_song)#Releases "Rebel Girl (Bikini Kill song)")* vinyl 7-inch EP for which she also played and sang back-up vocals. A CD single of "Let's Do It" featuring Jett and Westerberg was also released during this time, and appeared in the song credits for the movie *[Tank Girl](/wiki/Tank_Girl_(film) "Tank Girl (film)")*.

In 1997, Jett was featured on the *We Will Fall: The Iggy Pop Tribute* album. She performed a cover of the [Johnny O'Keefe](/wiki/Johnny_O%27Keefe "Johnny O'Keefe") song "[Wild One](/wiki/Wild_One_(Johnny_O%27Keefe_song) "Wild One (Johnny O'Keefe song)")" (or "Real Wild Child"). Jett is a guest artist on [Marky Ramone and the Intruders](/wiki/Marky_Ramone_and_the_Intruders "Marky Ramone and the Intruders")' 1999 album *The Answer to Your Problems?* on the track "Don't Blame Me". She is a guest vocalist on [Peaches](/wiki/Peaches_(musician) "Peaches (musician)")' album *[Impeach My Bush](/wiki/Impeach_My_Bush "Impeach My Bush")* on the tracks "[Boys Wanna Be Her](/wiki/Boys_Wanna_Be_Her "Boys Wanna Be Her")" and "You Love It".

In the 1990s she appeared in the [independent films](/wiki/Independent_film "Independent film") *The Sweet Life* and *[Boogie Boy](/wiki/Boogie_Boy "Boogie Boy")*. In 1992, she was a guest star in ["Free Fall"](/wiki/Highlander:_The_Series_(season_1)#ep05 "Highlander: The Series (season 1)"), a first-season episode of TV's *[Highlander: The Series](/wiki/Highlander:_The_Series "Highlander: The Series")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 1997, she appeared on the sitcom *[Ellen](/wiki/Ellen_(TV_series) "Ellen (TV series)")*, in the episode "Hello Muddah, Hello Faddah", performing the title song.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joan_Jett&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2000s")]

In 2000, Jett appeared in the Broadway production of *[The Rocky Horror Show](/wiki/The_Rocky_Horror_Show "The Rocky Horror Show")* in the role of Columbia. That same year, Jett appeared on *[Walker, Texas Ranger](/wiki/Walker,_Texas_Ranger "Walker, Texas Ranger")* as an ex-CIA agent turned assassin hired to kill Walker and Alex. In 2002, Jett appeared in the film *[By Hook or by Crook](/wiki/By_Hook_or_by_Crook_(2001_film) "By Hook or by Crook (2001 film)")* in the role of News Interviewee. From 2000 to 2003, Jett hosted a showcase of new film and video shorts, *Independent Eye*,[[35]](#cite_note-35) for [Maryland Public Television](/wiki/Maryland_Public_Television "Maryland Public Television").

In 2004, Jett and Laguna produced the album *[No Apologies](/wiki/No_Apologies_(The_Eyeliners_album) "No Apologies (The Eyeliners album)")* by the pop punk band [the Eyeliners](/wiki/The_Eyeliners "The Eyeliners"), after signing them. Jett also guested on the track "Destroy" and made a cameo appearance in its music video. Also in 2004, Jett narrated [Cam Archer](/wiki/Cam_Archer "Cam Archer")'s short film, *Godly Boyish*, about two teenagers who share suicidal fantasies.

In 2005, Jett and Laguna signed punk rockers the Vacancies and produced their second album, *A Beat Missing or a Silence Added* (reaching the top 20 in CMJ Music Charts), and their third album in 2007, *Tantrum*. That same year, she was recruited by [Steven Van Zandt](/wiki/Steven_Van_Zandt "Steven Van Zandt") to host her own radio show on Van Zandt's *[Underground Garage](/wiki/Underground_Garage "Underground Garage")* radio channel on [Sirius Satellite Radio](/wiki/Sirius_Satellite_Radio "Sirius Satellite Radio"). She hosted a four-hour show titled *Joan Jett's Radio Revolution*, broadcast every Saturday and Sunday.[[36]](#cite_note-36) The program moved from Sirius 25 (*Underground Garage*)[[37]](#cite_note-37) to Sirius 28 shortly before being canceled in June 2008.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) Also in 2005, Jett and Laguna celebrated the 25th anniversary of Blackheart Records with a sellout show at [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan")'s Webster Hall.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Joan_Jett_1.jpg/250px-Joan_Jett_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joan_Jett_1.jpg)

Jett performing in 2008

In June 2006, Jett released her album *[Sinner](/wiki/Sinner_(Joan_Jett_album) "Sinner (Joan Jett album)")*, on Blackheart Records. To support the album, the band appeared on the 2006 [Warped Tour](/wiki/Warped_Tour "Warped Tour") and on a fall 2006 tour with [Eagles of Death Metal](/wiki/Eagles_of_Death_Metal "Eagles of Death Metal"). Various other bands such as Antigone Rising, [Valient Thorr](/wiki/Valient_Thorr "Valient Thorr"), the Vacancies, Throw Rag and Riverboat Gamblers were to have joined the tour for a handful of dates each. Jett sang a duet with Chase Noles on "Tearstained Letters", a song on [the Heart Attacks](/wiki/The_Heart_Attacks "The Heart Attacks")' 2006 album, *Hellbound and Heartless*.

In 2008, Jett made a cameo appearance in [Darren Lynn Bousman](/wiki/Darren_Lynn_Bousman "Darren Lynn Bousman")'s rock opera/file *[Repo! The Genetic Opera](/wiki/Repo!_The_Genetic_Opera "Repo! The Genetic Opera")* as the guitarist in Shilo's room during the piece "Seventeen".[[41]](#cite_note-41) Also in 2008, she appeared in the *[Law & Order: Criminal Intent](/wiki/Law_%26_Order:_Criminal_Intent "Law & Order: Criminal Intent")* episode "[Reunion](/wiki/Reunion_(Law_%26_Order:_Criminal_Intent) "Reunion (Law & Order: Criminal Intent)")" as a rock-and-roll talk show host who is murdered.

### 2010s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joan_Jett&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2010s")]

Jett was an executive producer for the film *[The Runaways](/wiki/The_Runaways_(2010_film) "The Runaways (2010 film)")*, which chronicled the Runaways' career. It was written and directed by [Floria Sigismondi](/wiki/Floria_Sigismondi "Floria Sigismondi"), who has directed videos for [Marilyn Manson](/wiki/Marilyn_Manson_(band) "Marilyn Manson (band)"), [the White Stripes](/wiki/The_White_Stripes "The White Stripes") and [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie"). Production of the movie began filming around [*Twilight*](/wiki/Twilight_(2008_film) "Twilight (2008 film)")'s [Kristen Stewart](/wiki/Kristen_Stewart "Kristen Stewart")'s filming schedule, (i.e. of the sequels *[New Moon](/wiki/The_Twilight_Saga:_New_Moon "The Twilight Saga: New Moon")* and *[Eclipse](/wiki/The_Twilight_Saga:_Eclipse "The Twilight Saga: Eclipse")*). Stewart played Jett in the film. In order to prepare for the role, Stewart met Jett around January 2009. In an interview, Stewart revealed that she hoped to be able to sing some songs in the film.[[42]](#cite_note-42) The film explores the relationship between Jett and Runaways' lead singer, [Cherie Currie](/wiki/Cherie_Currie "Cherie Currie"), played by [Dakota Fanning](/wiki/Dakota_Fanning "Dakota Fanning"), and premiered at the 2010 Sundance Film Festival on January 24, 2010. Joan Jett and the Blackhearts appeared at the [2010 Sundance Film Festival](/wiki/2010_Sundance_Film_Festival "2010 Sundance Film Festival"), at Harry-O's, to promote the film.

Jett, along with the Blackhearts, released the album *[Unvarnished](/wiki/Unvarnished "Unvarnished")* on September 30, 2013. The album reached *Billboard*'s Top 50.[[43]](#cite_note-43) It included songs dealing with the death of her parents and other people.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) Joan Jett and the Blackhearts were inducted in the [Rock and Roll Hall of Fame](/wiki/Rock_and_Roll_Hall_of_Fame "Rock and Roll Hall of Fame") in 2015.[[46]](#cite_note-Hall_of_Fame-46)

Jett starred in and was the executive producer of the film *Undateable John*, which was released in 2014.[[47]](#cite_note-47) In April 2014, Jett fronted the remaining members of [Nirvana](/wiki/Nirvana_(band) "Nirvana (band)") for a performance of "[Smells Like Teen Spirit](/wiki/Smells_Like_Teen_Spirit "Smells Like Teen Spirit")" for their induction into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame. She joined the band again later that night for its surprise concert at [Saint Vitus](/wiki/Saint_Vitus_(venue) "Saint Vitus (venue)"). Jett played Betsy Neal in the television film *[Big Driver](/wiki/Big_Driver_(film) "Big Driver (film)")*. The film, based on [Stephen King](/wiki/Stephen_King "Stephen King")'s [novella of the same name](/wiki/Big_Driver "Big Driver"), premiered on [Lifetime](/wiki/Lifetime_(TV_network) "Lifetime (TV network)") on October 18, 2014. Jett provided the voice for the character Sunshine Justice in an episode of *[Steven Universe](/wiki/Steven_Universe "Steven Universe")*.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

### 2020s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joan_Jett&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2020s")]

Joan Jett and the Blackhearts released *Changeup* on March 25, 2022, the first acoustic album ever recorded by the band, featuring "Bad Reputation" and "Crimson and Clover".[[49]](#cite_note-49) On June 2, 2023, Joan Jett and the Blackhearts released the 6 song EP, *Mindsets*. The EP the band's first release of new material in ten years.[[50]](#cite_note-50) Jett played a post pandemic welcome back concert in honor of first responders in the summer of 2022 at the Nassau County [Harry Chapin](/wiki/Harry_Chapin "Harry Chapin") Lakeside Theatre hosted by Nassau County Executive [Bruce Blakeman](/wiki/Bruce_Blakeman "Bruce Blakeman") that set a record attendance of 27,000 concert attendees.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On May 14, 2021, it was announced that to celebrate the 40th anniversaries of Jett's first two albums, *Bad Reputation* and *I Love Rock & Roll*, Z2 Comics was releasing two [graphic novels](/wiki/Graphic_novels "Graphic novels") titled *Joan Jett and the Blackhearts - 40x40: Bad Reputation/I Love Rock 'n' Roll* that will bring Jett's "songs to life as 20 vivid stories" by female writers and artists in the comic book industry. The books were released in November 2021.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

## Other ventures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joan_Jett&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Other ventures")]

### Activism and philanthropy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joan_Jett&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Activism and philanthropy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Joan_Jett_by_David_Shankbone.jpg/250px-Joan_Jett_by_David_Shankbone.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joan_Jett_by_David_Shankbone.jpg)

Jett promoting [PETA](/wiki/People_for_the_Ethical_Treatment_of_Animals "People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals") in [Union Square](/wiki/Union_Square,_Manhattan "Union Square, Manhattan"), New York City, 2010

Jett has long supported animal rights activism and organizations such as [People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals](/wiki/People_for_the_Ethical_Treatment_of_Animals "People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals") (PETA).[[52]](#cite_note-52) For Valentine's Day 2022, PETA created a (Black)Heart-Shaped Pizza as a limited-time collaboration with a Los Angeles pizza shop, PIZZANISTA![[53]](#cite_note-53)

Jett worked with members of the punk rock band [the Gits](/wiki/The_Gits "The Gits"), whose lead singer and lyricist, [Mia Zapata](/wiki/Mia_Zapata "Mia Zapata"), had been raped and murdered in 1993.[[13]](#cite_note-VH1-13) The results of their collaboration was a live album, *[Evil Stig](/wiki/Evil_Stig "Evil Stig")* and a single, "Bob", whose earnings were contributed to the investigation of Zapata's murder. To this end, the band and Jett appeared on the television show *[America's Most Wanted](/wiki/America%27s_Most_Wanted "America's Most Wanted")*, appealing to the public for information. The case was solved in 2004, when Zapata's murderer, Jesus Mezquia, was brought to trial and convicted.

At an October 2001 [9/11](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") benefit in [Red Bank, New Jersey](/wiki/Red_Bank,_New_Jersey "Red Bank, New Jersey"),[[54]](#cite_note-54) Jett and Springsteen appeared together on stage for the first time and played "Light of Day".

Jett is a sports fan and has remained actively involved in the sports world. "Bad Reputation" was used by [Ultimate Fighting Championship](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship "Ultimate Fighting Championship")'s Women's Bantamweight Champion [Ronda Rousey](/wiki/Ronda_Rousey "Ronda Rousey") as her walkout song at the pay-per-view event [UFC 157](/wiki/UFC_157 "UFC 157") and is her current theme music in [WWE](/wiki/WWE "WWE"). In April 2019, Jett performed "Bad Reputation" at WWE's [WrestleMania 35](/wiki/WrestleMania_35 "WrestleMania 35") as Rousey was making her entrance.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

Jett's cover of "[Love Is All Around](/wiki/The_Mary_Tyler_Moore_Show_opening_sequence#Theme_song "The Mary Tyler Moore Show opening sequence")" (the theme song of *[The Mary Tyler Moore Show](/wiki/The_Mary_Tyler_Moore_Show "The Mary Tyler Moore Show")*) was used by the [NCAA](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") to promote the Women's Final Four, as well as the song "Unfinished Business", which was never commercially released. "Love Is All Around" gained substantial radio play and became the number one requested song without a supporting album. Jett supplied theme songs for the [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") [X Games](/wiki/X_Games "X Games") premiere and has contributed music to all their games since. At [Cal Ripken Jr.](/wiki/Cal_Ripken_Jr. "Cal Ripken Jr.")'s request she sang the U.S. national anthem at the [Baltimore Orioles](/wiki/Baltimore_Orioles "Baltimore Orioles") game in which he tied[[56]](#cite_note-56) [Lou Gehrig](/wiki/Lou_Gehrig "Lou Gehrig")'s record for consecutive games played. She also sang the national anthem at the final game played at Memorial Stadium. From 2006 to 2015, the melody for her song "I Hate Myself for Loving You" was used as the theme music for *[NBC Sunday Night Football](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football")* with re-worked lyrics and retitled "Waiting All Day for Sunday Night". Beginning with the 2019 season, Jett performs the song with Carrie Underwood in the opener of Sunday Night Football games.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

Though Jett supported [Howard Dean](/wiki/Howard_Dean "Howard Dean") in the [2004 election](/wiki/2004_United_States_presidential_election "2004 United States presidential election") because of his opposition to the [Iraq War](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War"),[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59) she has been a consistent supporter of the [United States Armed Forces](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces") throughout her career and has toured for the [United Service Organizations](/wiki/United_Service_Organizations "United Service Organizations") for over 20 years, and even performed at the [United States Military Academy](/wiki/United_States_Military_Academy "United States Military Academy").[[60]](#cite_note-60) She often explains that while she doesn't like war, she loves the military.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

In June 2016, as a response to the [Pulse nightclub shooting](/wiki/Pulse_nightclub_shooting "Pulse nightclub shooting") which left 49 dead, Jett was the first to sign [*Billboard* magazine's Open Letter on Gun Violence](/wiki/Billboard%27s_Open_Letter_on_Gun_Violence "Billboard's Open Letter on Gun Violence") demanding that Congress enact [gun control](/wiki/Gun_control "Gun control") legislation, specifically [universal background checks](/wiki/Universal_background_check "Universal background check"). Jett was followed by [Lady Gaga](/wiki/Lady_Gaga "Lady Gaga") and nearly 200 other musicians and music industry executives in their petition.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

In 2024, Jett contributed guitar to a re-release of [Mark Knopfler](/wiki/Mark_Knopfler "Mark Knopfler")'s "[Going Home: Theme of the Local Hero](/wiki/Going_Home:_Theme_of_the_Local_Hero "Going Home: Theme of the Local Hero")" in aid of the [Teenage Cancer Trust](/wiki/Teenage_Cancer_Trust "Teenage Cancer Trust").[[63]](#cite_note-63)

### Products and endorsements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joan_Jett&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Products and endorsements")]

In 2008, Gibson released the "Joan Jett Signature Melody Maker" guitar. It differs from Jett's model by having a single burstbucker 3 humbucking pickup, an ebony fretboard and a double-cutaway body in white with a black vinyl pickguard. It also features a kill switch in place of a pickup selector.[[64]](#cite_note-JJ-64) It retails for $839.[[64]](#cite_note-JJ-64) There is a "Blackheart" version of this guitar introduced in 2010. All specs are the same, but it is finished in black, with red and pearl heart inlays.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

On November 19, 2009, Mattel released a Joan Jett [Barbie](/wiki/Barbie "Barbie") doll. Her name and likeness were used with her permission.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

In June 2019, Gibson announced and released a third signature guitar for Jett, which is a wine-colored ES-339. The guitar was released after two years of research and development with Jett.[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68)

## Artistry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joan_Jett&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Artistry")]

Jett has a [mezzo-soprano](/wiki/Mezzo-soprano "Mezzo-soprano") vocal range.[[69]](#cite_note-69) Her signature guitar is a white [Gibson Melody Maker](/wiki/Gibson_Melody_Maker "Gibson Melody Maker"), which she has played on all her hits since 1977. Jett bought her first Melody Maker from [Eric Carmen](/wiki/Eric_Carmen "Eric Carmen"), following the breakup of the [Raspberries](/wiki/Raspberries_(band) "Raspberries (band)").[[70]](#cite_note-70) In regard to her white Melody Maker, the singer once stated: "In The Runaways I was using a [blond Les Paul](/wiki/Gibson_Les_Paul "Gibson Les Paul"). It's beautiful, and I still have it, but it's heavy as shit. I jump and run around a lot onstage, and it was really getting to my shoulder, so I was looking for a lighter guitar. I heard from one of our [road crew](/wiki/Road_crew "Road crew") that [Eric Carmen](/wiki/Eric_Carmen "Eric Carmen") from the [Raspberries](/wiki/Raspberries_(band) "Raspberries (band)") was selling a Melody Maker, so I ended up buying it. Now, this is the guitar that he played on "Go All the Way" and all those [Raspberries] hits. And then I played it on "I Love Rock 'n' Roll", "Crimson and Clover", "Do You Want to Touch Me", "Bad Reputation" ...all those early records. Then I took it off the road because I got nervous that someone was gonna steal it or break it. It's so beautiful. It's white, has no stickers on it, and there are cracks in the paint and yellowing from age or club cigarettes. It's an unbelievable-looking guitar. I have it in a closet and I take it out occasionally to record. But I don't even need to use it to record anymore, because I have a guitar that sounds pretty much like it. I'm actually kind of afraid to bring out the original. It's got a great heritage. It's a guitar full of hits."[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72)

Jett's [Gibson Melody Maker](/wiki/Gibson_Melody_Maker "Gibson Melody Maker") played during her tenure with the Runaways

The press touted Jett as the "Godmother of [Punk](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock")"[[73]](#cite_note-73) and the "Original [Riot Grrrl](/wiki/Riot_grrrl "Riot grrrl")". Jett has also been described as the Queen of Rock 'n' Roll.[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78) The [Riot Grrrl](/wiki/Riot_Grrrl "Riot Grrrl") movement started in the early 1990s, and many of its participants credited Jett as a role model and inspiration.

In 1983, musical satirist ["Weird Al" Yankovic](/wiki/%22Weird_Al%22_Yankovic "\"Weird Al\" Yankovic") released a parody of "[I Love Rock 'n' Roll](/wiki/I_Love_Rock_%27n%27_Roll#Joan_Jett_version "I Love Rock 'n' Roll")" entitled "[I Love Rocky Road](/wiki/I_Love_Rocky_Road "I Love Rocky Road")", changing the singer's passion for rock music with that for [ice cream](/wiki/Rocky_road_(ice_cream) "Rocky road (ice cream)"). In 1984 the comic strip *[Bloom County](/wiki/Bloom_County "Bloom County")* included a character named [Tess Turbo](/wiki/Minor_characters_in_Bloom_County#Tess_Turbo "Minor characters in Bloom County"), with a backing band called the Blackheads.[[79]](#cite_note-79) Jett enjoyed the joking sendup, saying "I actually bought the original artwork of that, and it's hanging in my office... I was honored he chose me to satirize."[[80]](#cite_note-80) Her name appears in the lyrics of the [Le Tigre](/wiki/Le_Tigre "Le Tigre") song "[Hot Topic](/wiki/Hot_Topic_(song) "Hot Topic (song)")", released in 1999.[[81]](#cite_note-81) The 1999 series *[Freaks and Geeks](/wiki/Freaks_and_Geeks "Freaks and Geeks")* used the song "Bad Reputation" as the opening theme.[[82]](#cite_note-82) In 2019 British blogger [LadBaby](/wiki/LadBaby "LadBaby") released a parody of "I love Rock 'n' Roll", changing the singer's passion for rock music with that for sausage rolls. "[I Love Sausage Rolls](/wiki/I_Love_Rock_%27n%27_Roll#LadBaby_version "I Love Rock 'n' Roll")" became the Christmas No.1 in the UK.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

In 2008, Gibson manufactured a signature model of her [Melody Maker](/wiki/Gibson_Melody_Maker "Gibson Melody Maker"), a white double cutaway with a zebra humbucker and "kill" toggle switch.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

In 2010, *[The Runaways](/wiki/The_Runaways_(2010_film) "The Runaways (2010 film)")*, a movie about her previous band, was released, starring [Kristen Stewart](/wiki/Kristen_Stewart "Kristen Stewart") as Jett and [Dakota Fanning](/wiki/Dakota_Fanning "Dakota Fanning") as Currie.[[13]](#cite_note-VH1-13) Jett was later the subject of a documentary, *[Bad Reputation](/wiki/Bad_Reputation_(2018_film) "Bad Reputation (2018 film)")*, which was released in theaters and streaming on September 28, 2018.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

Jett's honors include being inducted in the [Long Island Music Hall of Fame](/wiki/Long_Island_Music_Hall_of_Fame "Long Island Music Hall of Fame") (Class of 2006),[[86]](#cite_note-86) appearing on *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")*'s 100 greatest guitarists of all-time list in 2003 and 2023,[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88) and being named West Hollywood's Rock Legend in 2013.[[89]](#cite_note-89) She also received the Nanci Alexander Activist Award for her work on behalf of animal welfare.[[90]](#cite_note-90) In April 2014, Jett was the first woman to win the Golden God Award.[[91]](#cite_note-91) Former bandmates [Cherie Currie](/wiki/Cherie_Currie "Cherie Currie") and [Lita Ford](/wiki/Lita_Ford "Lita Ford") supported her. On April 24, 2014, *[Alternative Press](/wiki/Alternative_Press_(music_magazine) "Alternative Press (music magazine)")* magazine held its first-ever [Alternative Press Music Awards](/wiki/Alternative_Press_Music_Awards "Alternative Press Music Awards"), and Jett received the AP Icon Award.[[92]](#cite_note-92) In 2015, she and the Blackhearts were inducted into the [Rock and Roll Hall of Fame](/wiki/Rock_and_Roll_Hall_of_Fame "Rock and Roll Hall of Fame").[[46]](#cite_note-Hall_of_Fame-46)

In August 2024, [Virgin Atlantic](/wiki/Virgin_Atlantic "Virgin Atlantic") named an [Airbus A330-900](/wiki/Airbus_A330neo "Airbus A330neo") registered G-VRIF "Joan Jet" in her honour.[[93]](#cite_note-93) A trail was named after Jett in [Saint-Jean-d'Heurs](/wiki/Saint-Jean-d%27Heurs "Saint-Jean-d'Heurs"), a rural commune of [France](/wiki/France "France").[[94]](#cite_note-94)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joan_Jett&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Jett has been a vegetarian since the late 1980s and supports animal rights.[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96) She lives in [Long Beach, New York](/wiki/Long_Beach,_New_York "Long Beach, New York"), and has been a New York resident since the late 1970s.[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98) She is an avid [New York Liberty](/wiki/New_York_Liberty "New York Liberty") and [Baltimore Orioles](/wiki/Baltimore_Orioles "Baltimore Orioles") fan.[[99]](#cite_note-99)[[100]](#cite_note-100)

### Sexuality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joan_Jett&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Sexuality")]

For years Jett refused to either confirm or deny rumors that she is lesbian or [bisexual](/wiki/Bisexuality "Bisexuality"). In a 1994 interview with *[Out](/wiki/Out_(magazine) "Out (magazine)")* magazine she said, "I'm not saying no, I'm not saying yes, I'm saying believe what you want. Assume away — go ahead."[[101]](#cite_note-101) In 2006, she responded to an interviewer who had asked her when she had "come out" as a lesbian by saying, "I never made any kind of statement about my personal life on any level. I never made any proclamations. So I don't know where people are getting that from."[[102]](#cite_note-102)

In 2016, former Runaways guitarist [Lita Ford](/wiki/Lita_Ford "Lita Ford") revealed in her memoir that she temporarily quit the band because the other members were "all gay", saying "First I found out that [Sandy](/wiki/Sandy_West "Sandy West"), the one I had bonded with the most, was a lesbian. Then I found out that [Cherie](/wiki/Cherie_Currie "Cherie Currie") was messing around with Joan. I was so freaked out that I quit the band. When I found out that the girls were all gay in the band, I wasn't sure how to take it. I didn't know what it was."[[103]](#cite_note-103)

In a 2018 interview with *the New York Times*, when asked about how an LGBT film festival did not want to show her documentary because she was not "out", Jett said: "They don't want the movie there because I don't declare? [Holding up her necklace] What the [expletive] is that? Two [labryses](/wiki/Labrys "Labrys"), or axes, crossing each other, inside of two women's symbols crossing each other. It's not been off since I got it. And I wear this one every day. [She turns around, lifts her shirt and reveals a tattoo with similar female symbols on her lower back.] I don't know how much more you can declare."[[104]](#cite_note-104)

### Kim Fowley rape allegation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joan_Jett&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Kim Fowley rape allegation")]

In July 2015, attorney [Jackie Fuchs](/wiki/Jackie_Fox "Jackie Fox") (formerly Jackie Fox of [the Runaways](/wiki/The_Runaways "The Runaways")) alleged that [Kim Fowley](/wiki/Kim_Fowley "Kim Fowley") raped her on New Year's Eve 1975, at a party following a Runaways performance at an Orange County club. Sixteen years old at the time, Fuchs was reportedly given [Quaaludes](/wiki/Quaalude "Quaalude") by a man she thought was a roadie, and while she was incapacitated, Fowley allegedly raped her in full view of a group of partygoers and her bandmates Currie, West, and Jett; Ford was not present.[[105]](#cite_note-:0-105) *[Look Away](/wiki/Look_Away_(2021_film) "Look Away (2021 film)")*, a documentary about sexual abuse in the rock music industry features Fuchs' story.[[106]](#cite_note-106)

Fuchs said that her last memory of the night was seeing Currie and Jett staring at her as Fowley raped her.[[105]](#cite_note-:0-105) Kari Krome (co-founder and songwriter for the group) stated that she saw, "Jett and Currie sitting off to the side of the room for part of the time, snickering" during the rape.[[105]](#cite_note-:0-105) In 2015, Jett stated "Anyone who truly knows me understands that if I was aware of a friend or bandmate being violated, I would not stand by while it happened. For a group of young teenagers thrust into '70s rock stardom there were relationships that were bizarre, but I was not aware of this incident. Obviously Jackie's story is extremely upsetting and although we haven't spoken in decades, I wish her peace and healing."[[107]](#cite_note-107)[[108]](#cite_note-108) [Victory Tischler-Blue](/wiki/Victory_Tischler-Blue "Victory Tischler-Blue") (Fuchs's replacement in the group) said that all the members of the group "have always been aware of this ugly event".[[109]](#cite_note-109)
